figures.caption: Figure 4: Training pipeline of GeoVista. Stage I performs supervised fine-tuning on APEX-GRO trajectories. Stage II applies GRPO-based alignment on verifiable grounding and counting tasks. The plan reward in the figure is a simplified illustration; the exact state-machine formulation is described in the text.
Figure 8: Execution trajectory for the counting task.
Figure 9: Execution trajectory for the visual grounding task.
Figure 1: Comparison of active perception paradigms and performance.

Table 13: Experimental results on the XHR benchmark (Full Indicators). The overall tasks include visual perception, reasoning, and multi-turn dialogue tasks. Reasoning—AD=Anomaly (single-turn), FP=Future Prediction (multi-image), MRJC=Multi-region Joint Contrast (multi-image), MRJCS=Multi-region Joint Contrast (single-image, multi-box), OSJ=Object State Judgment (singleturn), Avg.=Reasoning average; Perception—COL=Color Detection, DET=Detection, SHP=Shape Recognition, OC=Object Classification, REL=Object Spatial Relationship, OCN=Object Counting, RCN=Regional Counting, OGD=Object Grounding, RG=Regional Grounding, Avg.=Perception average; Multi-turn—MTFP=Future Prediction, MAD=Anomaly, MOSJ=Object State Judgment, MTEM@1=multi-turn dialogue average.

Table 14: Experimental results on the LRS-VQA. The sub-tasks include Object Background (Bg.), Object Category (Cat.), Object Color (Color), Object Shape (Shape), Object Status (Stat.), Reasoning (Reason), and Rural or Urban (Rur./Urb.).
Figure 7: The visualization of examples from APEX-GRO dataset.
Table 6: Prompt for Hierarchical Counting CoT
Figure 5: Performance on UHR datasets. Black values represent our final scores across diverse sub-tasks, and red values highlight absolute improvements over baselines.

Figure 3: Data construction pipeline for APEX-GRO. The process consists of four stages: data collection, context construction, multi-turn execution, and quality control. Detailed prompt instructions are provided in Appendix A.
